outer-join.definition: An OUTER JOIN returns all rows from one table (the preserved table), plus matching rows from the other table. If there's no match, NULL values are returned for the other table's columns. Unlike INNER JOIN, you won't lose unmatched records.
outer-join.why_it_matters: OUTER JOINs are essential when you need to find records that don't have matches in another table, like customers who haven't placed orders, or products that haven't been sold. Without OUTER JOINs, you'd miss these important business insights. Real-world example: A marketing team needs to identify inactive customers for a re-engagement campaign.

examples.title: LEFT JOIN: Find all customers and their orders (including customers with no orders)
examples.explanation: This returns ALL customers. If a customer has orders, you see them. If not, order_id and total are NULL. This is the most common use case for outer joins.
examples.scenario: Marketing wants to send a 'come back' promotion to customers who haven't ordered in 6 months.

examples.title: Finding customers with NO orders using LEFT JOIN
examples.sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.email
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_id IS NULL;
examples.explanation: By filtering WHERE o.order_id IS NULL, we get only customers who have never placed an order. The NULL indicates no match was found.
examples.scenario: Sales team needs to identify leads who signed up but never purchased.

examples.title: FULL OUTER JOIN: All records from both tables (MySQL simulated)
examples.sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, o.order_id
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
UNION
SELECT c.customer_name, o.order_id
FROM customers c
RIGHT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;
examples.explanation: MySQL doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN directly. This UNION pattern combines LEFT and RIGHT joins to get all records from both tables.
examples.scenario: Data reconciliation: finding all customers and all orders, including orphans from both sides.

common_mistakes.title: Using WHERE to filter the joined table instead of ON
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT c.name, o.total
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.total > 100;
common_mistakes.error_message: No error, but silently removes customers with no orders or orders <= 100
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The WHERE clause filters AFTER the join is complete, turning your LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN for the filtered results. Customers without matching orders are excluded because o.total IS NULL, which fails the > 100 check.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Filter the joined table in the ON clause, not the WHERE clause, when using OUTER JOINs to preserve unmatched rows.

common_mistakes.title: Expecting INNER JOIN behavior from OUTER JOIN
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT c.name, o.total
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date > '2024-01-01';
common_mistakes.error_message: Results show only customers with recent orders - missing customers with no orders or old orders
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Developers often write LEFT JOIN out of habit but then filter in WHERE, not realizing they're defeating the purpose of the outer join.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Ask yourself: Do I want all rows from the first table? If yes, don't filter the second table in WHERE.

reflection_prompts: Why would a business prefer to see NULL values instead of just excluding unmatched records?
How would you explain the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN to a non-technical manager?
When might a RIGHT JOIN be more natural than a LEFT JOIN? (Think about table order)
Can you think of a business scenario where knowing what ISN'T there is more valuable than knowing what is?
practice_suggestions: Write a query to find all products that have never been ordered
List all employees and their department names, including employees without assigned departments
Create a report showing monthly sales with months that had zero sales included
Find suppliers who haven't provided any products in the last year

self-join.definition: A SELF JOIN is when a table is joined to itself. You create two aliases (like employee e1 and employee e2) to treat the same table as if it were two separate tables, allowing you to compare rows within the same table.
self-join.why_it_matters: SELF JOINs solve hierarchical and comparative problems that exist within a single table. They're essential for organizational charts (employees and managers), finding pairs of related items, and comparing records to each other. Without self-joins, you'd need complex application code to process these relationships.

examples.title: Employee-Manager hierarchy: Show each employee with their manager's name
examples.sql:
SELECT e.name AS employee_name,
       m.name AS manager_name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
examples.explanation: We join the employees table to itself. 'e' represents employees, 'm' represents managers (who are also employees). The LEFT JOIN includes the CEO who has no manager.
examples.scenario: HR needs an organizational chart showing reporting relationships.

examples.title: Finding employees who earn more than their managers
examples.sql:
SELECT e.name AS employee,
       e.salary AS emp_salary,
       m.name AS manager,
       m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
examples.explanation: This compares salary values between related rows in the same table. Great for finding policy violations or promotion candidates.
examples.scenario: HR audit to identify employees who may have been over-promoted or managers who need raises.

examples.title: Finding pairs of products in the same category
examples.sql:
SELECT p1.name AS product1,
       p2.name AS product2,
       p1.category
FROM products p1
JOIN products p2 ON p1.category = p2.category AND p1.id < p2.id;
examples.explanation: We join products to themselves where they share a category. The p1.id < p2.id prevents duplicate pairs and self-pairing.
examples.scenario: Product team wants to identify similar products for cross-selling recommendations.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to use table aliases
common_mistakes.error_sql: SELECT name FROM employees JOIN employees ON manager_id = id;
common_mistakes.error_message: Column 'name' in field list is ambiguous
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Without aliases (e1, e2), MySQL can't distinguish which table's 'name' column you want. Both tables in the join have the same columns.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always use meaningful aliases (e for employee, m for manager) when self-joining.

common_mistakes.title: Creating a Cartesian product with missing join condition
common_mistakes.error_message: Query runs but returns every employee paired with every other employee (n² rows)
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Without the ON clause linking the relationship (like manager_id = id), SQL performs a cross join.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Self-joins MUST have a join condition that defines the relationship between the two 'copies' of the table.

reflection_prompts: Why do we need to use aliases for a self-join but not for joining two different tables?
What would happen if you used = instead of < in the product pairing query?
How would you modify the employee-manager query to show the full chain from CEO to intern?
Can you think of other real-world scenarios where data references other data in the same collection?
practice_suggestions: Find all customers who live in the same city as another customer
List employees who were hired on the same day
Create a query to find products that have the same price
Build a recursive-style report showing 3 levels of management (employee → manager → director)

having-clause.definition: The HAVING clause filters groups of rows AFTER aggregation has occurred. While WHERE filters individual rows before grouping, HAVING filters the results of GROUP BY based on aggregate values like COUNT, SUM, or AVG.
having-clause.why_it_matters: HAVING is essential for answering questions about groups that meet certain criteria. Without it, you'd have to fetch all aggregated data into your application and filter there, which is inefficient. Use cases include finding customers with multiple orders, departments with high average salaries, or products with low sales volumes.

examples.title: Find customers with 5 or more orders
examples.sql:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5;
examples.explanation: WHERE can't filter on COUNT(*) because aggregation hasn't happened yet. HAVING filters after groups are formed and counted.
examples.scenario: Identify VIP customers for a loyalty program.

examples.title: Departments with above-average salary
examples.sql:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
examples.explanation: HAVING can use subqueries and compare aggregate values. This finds departments paying better than the company average.
examples.scenario: Compensation analysis for departmental budget adjustments.

examples.title: Combining WHERE and HAVING
examples.sql:
SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
WHERE status = 'active'
GROUP BY category
HAVING AVG(price) > 50;
examples.explanation: WHERE first filters to active products only, then GROUP BY creates categories, then HAVING filters categories with high average prices.
examples.scenario: Pricing analysis: which active product categories are premium-priced?

common_mistakes.title: Using WHERE with aggregate functions
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT department, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
WHERE COUNT(*) > 5
GROUP BY department;
common_mistakes.error_message: Invalid use of group function
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: WHERE executes before GROUP BY, so aggregate values don't exist yet. You can't count groups before groups exist.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
SELECT department, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: WHERE filters rows; HAVING filters groups. Use HAVING for conditions on COUNT, SUM, AVG, etc.

common_mistakes.title: Putting non-aggregated column filters in HAVING
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT category, AVG(price)
FROM products
GROUP BY category
HAVING status = 'active';
common_mistakes.error_message: Unknown column 'status' in 'having clause'
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Individual row conditions (like status='active') should be in WHERE. HAVING is only for aggregate conditions.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
SELECT category, AVG(price)
FROM products
WHERE status = 'active'
GROUP BY category;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Filter individual row values in WHERE (more efficient), aggregate values in HAVING.

reflection_prompts: Why does SQL have both WHERE and HAVING instead of just one filtering mechanism?
What would happen if you put a column filter in HAVING that isn't in the GROUP BY?
Can you use HAVING without GROUP BY? What would it do?
How would you find the second-highest order total by customer?
practice_suggestions: Find products that have been ordered more than 100 times
List months with total sales exceeding $10,000
Identify customers who haven't ordered in 6+ months (use HAVING with date functions)
Find categories where the most expensive product costs more than 10x the cheapest

correlated-subquery.definition: A correlated subquery is a subquery that references columns from the outer query. Unlike regular subqueries that run once, correlated subqueries execute once for EACH row in the outer query, making them slower but more powerful for row-by-row comparisons.
correlated-subquery.why_it_matters: Correlated subqueries solve problems where each row needs to be evaluated against a dynamic set of data that depends on that row's values. Classic examples: finding the most recent order per customer, employees with above-department-average salary, or products with above-category-average pricing. They're often the clearest way to express 'for each X, find the Y that...' logic.

examples.title: Find employees earning above their department average
examples.sql:
SELECT name, department, salary
FROM employees e1
WHERE salary > (
    SELECT AVG(salary)
    FROM employees e2
    WHERE e2.department = e1.department
);
examples.explanation: For each employee (e1), the subquery calculates the average salary of their specific department (e2). The correlation is e2.department = e1.department.
examples.scenario: Performance review to identify top performers relative to peers.

examples.title: Most recent order for each customer
examples.sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, o.order_id, o.order_date
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date = (
    SELECT MAX(order_date)
    FROM orders o2
    WHERE o2.customer_id = c.customer_id
);
examples.explanation: The correlated subquery finds the latest order date for each specific customer, then we join to get the full order details.
examples.scenario: Customer service dashboard showing each customer's last interaction.

examples.title: Products priced above category average
examples.sql:
SELECT name, category, price
FROM products p1
WHERE price > (
    SELECT AVG(price)
    FROM products p2
    WHERE p2.category = p1.category
);
examples.explanation: Each product is compared against the average of its own category. This identifies premium products within each category.
examples.scenario: Product marketing: identifying premium items for targeted promotions.

common_mistakes.title: Using uncorrelated subquery when correlation is needed
common_mistakes.error_message: No error, but returns employees above COMPANY average, not department average
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The subquery runs once and returns the overall average. To get department-specific averages, you need correlation.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
SELECT name, department, salary
FROM employees e1
WHERE salary > (
    SELECT AVG(salary)
    FROM employees e2
    WHERE e2.department = e1.department
);
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Add the table alias correlation (e2.dept = e1.dept) when you need per-group calculations.

common_mistakes.title: Performance issues with large datasets
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT * FROM large_table l1
WHERE col > (SELECT AVG(col) FROM large_table l2 WHERE l2.group_id = l1.group_id);
common_mistakes.error_message: Query times out or runs very slowly on millions of rows
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Correlated subqueries execute once per row (O(n²)). On large tables, this is prohibitively slow.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
WITH avg_by_group AS (
    SELECT group_id, AVG(col) AS avg_col
    FROM large_table
    GROUP BY group_id
)
SELECT l.* FROM large_table l
JOIN avg_by_group a ON l.group_id = a.group_id
WHERE l.col > a.avg_col;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: For large tables, use JOINs or CTEs instead of correlated subqueries for better performance.

reflection_prompts: How many times does a correlated subquery execute compared to a non-correlated one?
Can you rewrite the 'above department average' query using a JOIN instead? Which is clearer?
What happens if the correlated subquery returns no rows for a given outer row?
When would you choose clarity (correlated subquery) over performance (JOIN)?
practice_suggestions: Find customers whose last order total was above their average order total
List employees who were hired first in their department
Identify products with inventory below the average for their category
Find students who scored above average in each subject they took

create-table.definition: CREATE TABLE defines a new table structure including column names, data types, constraints, and indexes. It's the foundation of database design - you specify not just what data to store, but how it should behave through constraints like PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, and FOREIGN KEY.
create-table.why_it_matters: Proper table design determines data integrity, query performance, and application reliability for years. A well-designed table with appropriate constraints prevents bad data at the source, while a poorly designed one leads to data quality issues that are expensive to fix later. CREATE TABLE is where you enforce business rules directly in the database.

examples.title: Basic table with constraints
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE customers (
    customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    status ENUM('active', 'inactive') DEFAULT 'active'
);
examples.explanation: Shows PRIMARY KEY (unique identifier), AUTO_INCREMENT (auto-numbering), NOT NULL (required), UNIQUE (no duplicates), DEFAULT values, and ENUM (restricted values).
examples.scenario: Creating a customer table that enforces data quality from day one.

examples.title: Table with foreign key relationships
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    customer_id INT NOT NULL,
    order_date DATE NOT NULL,
    total DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (total >= 0),
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
examples.explanation: Foreign keys ensure referential integrity. ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents deleting customers with orders. CHECK constraint ensures non-negative totals.
examples.scenario: Order table that maintains data consistency with customers.

examples.title: Table with indexes for performance
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    sku VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    category_id INT,
    price DECIMAL(10,2),
    INDEX idx_category (category_id),
    INDEX idx_price (price),
    FULLTEXT INDEX idx_name (name)
);
examples.explanation: Multiple index types: regular INDEX for filtering/sorting, FULLTEXT for text search. Indexes speed up queries but slow down writes.
examples.scenario: Product catalog optimized for search and filtering performance.

common_mistakes.title: Missing PRIMARY KEY
common_mistakes.error_sql: CREATE TABLE logs (message TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP);
common_mistakes.error_message: No error, but table has no unique identifier
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Beginners often skip PRIMARY KEY thinking it's optional. Every table should have a way to uniquely identify rows.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always include a PRIMARY KEY, usually an auto-incrementing integer named id or table_id.

common_mistakes.title: Wrong data type for values
common_mistakes.error_sql: CREATE TABLE products (price VARCHAR(10), quantity FLOAT);
common_mistakes.error_message: No immediate error, but calculations fail and precision is lost
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Using VARCHAR for money prevents math operations. FLOAT causes rounding errors in financial calculations.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Use DECIMAL for money (exact precision), INT for whole numbers, appropriate types for your data.

reflection_prompts: Why is it better to enforce data rules in the database rather than just in the application?
What are the trade-offs of adding many indexes to a table?
When would you use UUID instead of AUTO_INCREMENT for a primary key?
How would you design a table to support soft deletes instead of hard deletes?
practice_suggestions: Design a blog system with users, posts, comments, and categories
Create an e-commerce schema with products, inventory, carts, and orders
Build a library management system with books, members, loans, and fines
Design a social media schema with users, posts, likes, and follows

transactions.definition: A transaction is a group of SQL operations that succeed or fail together as a single unit. Using START TRANSACTION (or BEGIN), COMMIT, and ROLLBACK, you ensure database consistency even if errors occur midway through a multi-step operation.
transactions.why_it_matters: Transactions are essential for maintaining data integrity in real-world applications. Bank transfers, inventory updates, and order processing all require multiple related changes. Without transactions, a system crash midway could leave data in an inconsistent state - money deducted but not transferred, inventory reduced but order not created. ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) guarantee reliable processing.

examples.title: Basic bank transfer transaction
examples.explanation: Both updates succeed or both fail. If account 2 doesn't exist, ROLLBACK would restore account 1's balance.
examples.scenario: ATM transfer: ensuring money isn't lost in transit.

examples.title: Transaction with error handling
examples.explanation: Multi-step order creation. If inventory update fails or order insert fails, everything is rolled back.
examples.scenario: E-commerce checkout: inventory, order, and items must all succeed.

examples.title: Savepoints for partial rollback
examples.explanation: SAVEPOINT creates a marker within a transaction. You can roll back to it without aborting the entire transaction.
examples.scenario: Complex workflows where some operations can be retried without starting over.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting COMMIT
common_mistakes.error_message: Changes appear in your session but disappear when you disconnect; other users don't see changes
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Transactions are auto-committed when session ends in some modes, but in strict mode, uncommitted changes are lost.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Every START TRANSACTION needs a matching COMMIT or ROLLBACK.

common_mistakes.title: Holding transactions open too long
common_mistakes.error_message: Performance degradation, deadlocks, lock wait timeouts for other users
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Locks are held for the entire transaction duration. Long transactions block other operations.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Transactions should be as short as possible. Get in, do work, commit immediately.

reflection_prompts: What would happen to a bank account balance if a transfer crashed midway without transactions?
When would you use ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT instead of full ROLLBACK?
Why can't you roll back a committed transaction? What should you do instead?
How do transactions interact with other concurrent transactions?
practice_suggestions: Implement a funds transfer between two accounts with proper error handling
Create a stored procedure for order placement that handles inventory, orders, and items atomically
Design a reservation system where seats are held temporarily then released or confirmed
Build a batch processing system that can resume from failures

isolation-levels.definition: Transaction isolation levels control how much transactions can see each other's uncommitted changes. They balance between data consistency (seeing accurate data) and concurrency (allowing multiple transactions to run simultaneously). MySQL supports READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ (default), and SERIALIZABLE.
isolation-levels.why_it_matters: Different applications need different consistency guarantees. A financial system needs strict consistency (SERIALIZABLE), while a reporting dashboard might prioritize performance (READ COMMITTED). Understanding isolation levels helps you choose the right trade-off between accuracy and speed, and debug mysterious data anomalies like phantom reads or non-repeatable reads.

examples.title: REPEATABLE READ - Default MySQL behavior
examples.explanation: Within a transaction, you see a consistent snapshot as of the transaction start. Prevents non-repeatable reads.
examples.scenario: Report generation: ensuring all numbers in a complex report come from the same point in time.

examples.title: READ COMMITTED - See committed changes immediately
examples.explanation: Each statement sees the latest committed data. Good balance of consistency and concurrency.
examples.scenario: Web application: users should see their own changes immediately after commit, but don't need strict snapshot consistency.

examples.title: SERIALIZABLE - Strictest isolation
examples.explanation: Transactions are completely isolated as if they ran sequentially. Locks the range of data being queried.
examples.scenario: Financial audit: ensuring no new transactions appear mid-audit that would affect totals.

common_mistakes.title: Phantom reads with the wrong isolation level
common_mistakes.error_message: Application shows stale data; new records don't appear until transaction ends
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: REPEATABLE READ prevents seeing changes made by other transactions within your transaction.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Choose READ COMMITTED if you need to see other transactions' committed changes during your transaction.

common_mistakes.title: Deadlocks at SERIALIZABLE level
common_mistakes.error_message: Deadlock found when trying to get lock; try restarting transaction
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: SERIALIZABLE holds locks more aggressively. Two transactions waiting for each other's locks cause deadlock.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Higher isolation = more locking = more deadlock risk. Only use SERIALIZABLE when truly necessary.

reflection_prompts: Why might a banking system prefer SERIALIZABLE while a social media feed might use READ COMMITTED?
What's the difference between a dirty read and a non-repeatable read?
Can you have data integrity issues even with the highest isolation level?
How does MySQL's default REPEATABLE READ differ from PostgreSQL's default READ COMMITTED?
practice_suggestions: Set up two concurrent transactions and observe behavior at different isolation levels
Design an inventory system that prevents overselling using appropriate isolation
Create a reporting system that generates consistent snapshots of related tables
Implement optimistic locking using version numbers as an alternative to high isolation levels

views.definition: A VIEW is a virtual table defined by a stored query. It doesn't store data itself but presents data from underlying tables in a customized way. Views can simplify complex queries, restrict data access, and provide backward compatibility when table structures change.
views.why_it_matters: Views are powerful abstractions that make databases more maintainable and secure. They let you encapsulate complex joins and calculations, expose only necessary columns to users, and create stable interfaces even when underlying tables evolve. Use views to create 'report-ready' datasets, implement row-level security, and hide schema complexity from applications.

examples.title: Simple view for active customers
examples.sql:
CREATE VIEW active_customers AS
SELECT customer_id, name, email, created_at
FROM customers
WHERE status = 'active'
    AND deleted_at IS NULL;
examples.explanation: Applications can query active_customers without knowing the status/deleted_at filter logic. If the business rules change, only the view needs updating.
examples.scenario: Application always works with active customers; logic is centralized in the view.

examples.title: Complex view with joins and calculations
examples.sql:
CREATE VIEW customer_order_summary AS
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders,
    COALESCE(SUM(o.total), 0) AS total_spent,
    MAX(o.order_date) AS last_order_date,
    AVG(o.total) AS avg_order_value
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.name;
examples.explanation: Encapsulates complex aggregation logic. Reports can SELECT * FROM customer_order_summary without writing the join and GROUP BY every time.
examples.scenario: Dashboard showing customer lifetime value and engagement metrics.

examples.title: Updatable view with WITH CHECK OPTION
examples.explanation: WITH CHECK OPTION ensures inserted/updated rows satisfy the view's WHERE clause. Prevents 'disappearing' rows that don't match the view filter.
examples.scenario: Regional applications that should only work with their region's data.

common_mistakes.title: Trying to update a non-updatable view
common_mistakes.error_message: The target table customer_totals of the UPDATE is not updatable
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Views with GROUP BY, DISTINCT, aggregate functions, or certain joins can't be updated - MySQL doesn't know which underlying rows to modify.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Only simple views (single table, no aggregates, no DISTINCT) are updatable.

common_mistakes.title: Performance issues with complex views
common_mistakes.error_sql:
CREATE VIEW all_customer_data AS
SELECT c.*, o.*, p.*, a.*
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.id = p.order_id
LEFT JOIN addresses a ON c.id = a.customer_id;

SELECT name FROM all_customer_data WHERE id = 5;
common_mistakes.error_message: Slow query - all joins execute even though only customer data is needed
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The view's full query runs first, then filters. MySQL may not optimize out unnecessary joins.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Views don't cache results. Complex views can cause performance issues if used carelessly.

reflection_prompts: When would you choose a VIEW over a regular table for reporting?
How do views help with database security and access control?
What happens to a view if you rename or drop an underlying table column?
Can views help with database refactoring without breaking applications?
practice_suggestions: Create views for common reporting needs in an e-commerce database
Design a view that implements row-level security (users only see their data)
Build views that present the same data in different ways for different departments
Create a view that formats data for API consumption (JSON-like structure)

stored-procedures.definition: A stored procedure is a pre-compiled SQL program stored in the database. It can accept parameters, contain control flow (IF, LOOP), execute multiple statements, and return results. Procedures encapsulate business logic directly in the database.
stored-procedures.why_it_matters: Stored procedures reduce network traffic, centralize business logic, and improve security by limiting direct table access. They're essential for complex operations that must be atomic, like order processing with inventory checks. They also enable database APIs that applications call rather than writing raw SQL.

examples.title: Simple procedure with parameters
examples.sql:
DELIMITER //
CREATE PROCEDURE GetCustomerOrders(IN cust_id INT)
BEGIN
    SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, o.total
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.customer_id = cust_id
    ORDER BY o.order_date DESC;
END //
DELIMITER ;

-- Call it:
CALL GetCustomerOrders(123);
examples.explanation: IN parameter receives a value. The procedure encapsulates the query logic. Applications call the procedure instead of writing the SQL.
examples.scenario: Standardized way to fetch customer order history across all applications.

examples.title: Procedure with output parameter and logic
examples.explanation: OUT parameter returns a value. Shows variable assignment, INTO clause, and conditional logic.
examples.scenario: Centralized business logic for order total calculation with tax, discounts, etc.

examples.title: Complex procedure with transaction handling
examples.explanation: Shows variables, transaction control, error handling with SIGNAL, and multi-step business logic.
examples.scenario: E-commerce checkout: inventory check, order creation, and stock update as atomic operation.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting DELIMITER change
common_mistakes.error_message: ERROR 1064: You have an error in your SQL syntax near ''
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The semicolon inside the procedure body terminates the CREATE PROCEDURE statement prematurely.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always change DELIMITER before creating procedures with multiple statements.

common_mistakes.title: Variable name conflicts with column names
common_mistakes.error_message: No error, but may return unexpected results or all rows (column user_id = column user_id is always true)
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: MySQL can't distinguish between the parameter 'user_id' and the column 'user_id'.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Use naming conventions like p_ prefix for parameters to avoid conflicts.

reflection_prompts: When is a stored procedure better than application code? When is it worse?
How do stored procedures help with database security?
What are the challenges of version controlling stored procedures?
How would you debug a stored procedure that's not working correctly?
practice_suggestions: Create a procedure to transfer funds between accounts with validation
Build a reporting procedure that accepts date range parameters
Write a procedure to archive old data to a history table
Design a user registration procedure with email uniqueness checking

triggers.definition: A trigger is a stored program that automatically executes (fires) when a specific event occurs on a table - BEFORE or AFTER an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. Triggers enforce rules automatically without application involvement.
triggers.why_it_matters: Triggers ensure data integrity rules are followed regardless of which application modifies the data. They're perfect for audit logging (tracking who changed what), maintaining derived values, enforcing complex constraints, and cascading operations. However, they can make debugging harder and add hidden complexity.

examples.title: Auto-update timestamp on row modification
examples.explanation: NEW refers to the row being modified. BEFORE UPDATE runs before the change is saved. Every UPDATE on customers automatically updates the timestamp.
examples.scenario: Audit trail: tracking when records were last modified without application code.

examples.title: Audit log trigger
examples.sql:
CREATE TRIGGER log_customer_changes
AFTER UPDATE ON customers
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO customer_audit_log (
        customer_id,
        changed_at,
        old_email,
        new_email,
        old_status,
        new_status
    ) VALUES (
        OLD.customer_id,
        NOW(),
        OLD.email,
        NEW.email,
        OLD.status,
        NEW.status
    );
END;
examples.explanation: AFTER UPDATE runs after the change. OLD contains pre-change values, NEW contains post-change values. Creates a complete change history.
examples.scenario: Compliance requirements: maintaining complete audit trail of customer data changes.

examples.title: Enforce business rule before insert
examples.explanation: BEFORE INSERT can validate data and raise errors. It can also modify values (NEW.requires_approval) before they're saved.
examples.scenario: Business rule enforcement: large orders require approval flag automatically set.

common_mistakes.title: Recursive trigger causing infinite loop
common_mistakes.error_message: ERROR 1442: Can't update table 'orders' in stored function/trigger because it is already used by statement
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The trigger updates the same table it's watching, which would fire itself again infinitely.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
-- Use BEFORE UPDATE to modify values instead
CREATE TRIGGER update_total
BEFORE UPDATE ON orders
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    SET NEW.total = NEW.total + 1;
END;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Can't modify the same table in an AFTER trigger. Use BEFORE triggers to change NEW values.

common_mistakes.title: Mutating table error in complex triggers
common_mistakes.error_message: Error reading from orders table while inserting (in some MySQL versions)
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Reading from the same table you're modifying can cause consistency issues.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
-- Query a different table or use a stored procedure instead of trigger
-- Or track count in a separate summary table updated by trigger
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Avoid querying the trigger's own table. Use summary tables or application logic instead.

reflection_prompts: What are the pros and cons of using triggers versus application-level validation?
How can triggers make debugging difficult?
When would you use BEFORE vs AFTER triggers?
What happens if a trigger fails? How does it affect the original operation?
practice_suggestions: Create an audit log system that tracks all changes to sensitive tables
Build a trigger that maintains a denormalized count in a parent table
Write a trigger that validates data across multiple tables
Design a soft-delete system using triggers (set deleted_at instead of DELETE)

window-functions.definition: Window functions perform calculations across a set of table rows that are related to the current row - called the 'window' or 'partition'. Unlike aggregate functions with GROUP BY, window functions don't collapse rows; each row keeps its identity while gaining new calculated columns like row numbers, running totals, or rankings.
window-functions.why_it_matters: Window functions solve complex analytical queries that were previously difficult or impossible in SQL. Running totals, moving averages, rankings, and year-over-year comparisons become straightforward. They're essential for reporting, analytics, and business intelligence without needing to export data to Excel or Python.

examples.title: ROW_NUMBER for top-N per group
examples.sql:
SELECT 
    department,
    name,
    salary,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM employees;
examples.explanation: PARTITION BY divides data into groups (departments). ORDER BY sorts within each group. ROW_NUMBER assigns 1, 2, 3... within each partition.
examples.scenario: Find top 3 highest-paid employees in each department.

examples.title: Running total with cumulative SUM
examples.sql:
SELECT 
    order_date,
    daily_total,
    SUM(daily_total) OVER (
        ORDER BY order_date
        ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING
    ) AS running_total
FROM daily_sales;
examples.explanation: ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING means 'sum all rows from the start up to current row'. Creates a running total without self-joins.
examples.scenario: Cumulative revenue report showing growth over time.

examples.title: LAG and LEAD for period comparison
examples.sql:
SELECT 
    month,
    revenue,
    LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month,
    revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS month_change,
    ROUND(
        (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) / LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) * 100,
        2
    ) AS pct_change
FROM monthly_revenue;
examples.explanation: LAG gets the value from the previous row. LEAD gets the next row's value. Essential for period-over-period analysis.
examples.scenario: Monthly business report showing growth rate and trends.

common_mistakes.title: Using window functions in WHERE clause
common_mistakes.error_sql:
SELECT name, salary,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees
WHERE salary_rank <= 10;
common_mistakes.error_message: Unknown column 'salary_rank' in 'where clause'
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Window functions execute after WHERE filtering. The alias isn't available when WHERE runs.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Use a subquery or CTE to filter on window function results.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing RANK, DENSE_RANK, and ROW_NUMBER
common_mistakes.error_message: May return 4+ rows if there are ties for 3rd place
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: RANK gives same rank to ties and skips numbers (1, 2, 2, 4). ROW_NUMBER is sequential (1, 2, 3, 4). DENSE_RANK doesn't skip (1, 2, 2, 3).
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
-- Use ROW_NUMBER for exactly N rows
-- Use RANK for all tied with 3rd place
-- Use DENSE_RANK for top 3 distinct scores
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Choose the right ranking function: ROW_NUMBER (exact count), RANK (allow ties, skip numbers), DENSE_RANK (allow ties, no skips).

reflection_prompts: How would you calculate a 7-day moving average using window functions?
What's the difference between PARTITION BY and GROUP BY?
When would you use window functions instead of self-joins?
How do you handle ties when selecting 'top N' with window functions?
practice_suggestions: Calculate percentiles (median, 90th percentile) for employee salaries
Build a query showing each employee's salary relative to their department average
Create a running balance report for an account transaction history
Find consecutive days with above-average sales (streak detection)

cte.definition: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) are named temporary result sets defined with the WITH clause. They make complex queries more readable by breaking them into named, logical steps. CTEs can be referenced multiple times in the main query and can even be recursive for hierarchical data.
cte.why_it_matters: CTEs transform unreadable nested subqueries into clear, step-by-step logic. They improve code maintainability, enable recursive queries (for org charts, bill of materials), and often optimize better than equivalent subqueries. They're essential for modern SQL development.

examples.title: Simple CTE for query organization
examples.sql:
WITH high_value_orders AS (
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, total
    FROM orders
    WHERE total > 1000
),
customer_totals AS (
    SELECT 
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        SUM(total) AS spent
    FROM high_value_orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.name, ct.order_count, ct.spent
FROM customers c
JOIN customer_totals ct ON c.customer_id = ct.customer_id
WHERE ct.order_count >= 3;
examples.explanation: Each CTE builds on previous ones logically. Much clearer than nested subqueries. high_value_orders filters first, then customer_totals aggregates.
examples.scenario: Find VIP customers with 3+ high-value orders for premium support.

examples.title: Recursive CTE for hierarchical data
examples.sql:
WITH RECURSIVE employee_hierarchy AS (
    -- Anchor member: top-level employees
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level
    FROM employees
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive member: employees with managers
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, eh.level + 1
    FROM employees e
    JOIN employee_hierarchy eh ON e.manager_id = eh.id
)
SELECT 
    REPEAT('  ', level) || name AS org_chart,
    level
FROM employee_hierarchy
ORDER BY level, name;
examples.explanation: Anchor finds starting points (CEO). Recursive part keeps joining until no more matches. Builds complete hierarchy tree.
examples.scenario: Generate organizational chart showing reporting structure with indentation.

examples.title: CTE with multiple references
examples.sql:
WITH monthly_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        DATE_FORMAT(order_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        AVG(total) AS avg_order,
        SUM(total) AS revenue
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY DATE_FORMAT(order_date, '%Y-%m')
)
SELECT 
    m1.month,
    m1.revenue,
    m2.revenue AS prev_month_revenue,
    ROUND((m1.revenue - m2.revenue) / m2.revenue * 100, 2) AS growth_pct
FROM monthly_stats m1
LEFT JOIN monthly_stats m2
    ON m2.month = DATE_FORMAT(DATE_SUB(STR_TO_DATE(CONCAT(m1.month, '-01'), '%Y-%m-%d'), INTERVAL 1 MONTH), '%Y-%m')
ORDER BY m1.month;
examples.explanation: CTE is defined once but can be referenced multiple times (m1, m2). Self-joining the CTE for month-over-month comparison.
examples.scenario: Monthly growth report comparing revenue to previous month.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting RECURSIVE keyword
common_mistakes.error_sql:
WITH employee_hierarchy AS (
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level
    FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, eh.level + 1
    FROM employees e
    JOIN employee_hierarchy eh ON e.manager_id = eh.id
) SELECT * FROM employee_hierarchy;
common_mistakes.error_message: Recursive Common Table Expression 'employee_hierarchy' should contain exactly one UNION/UNION ALL
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Without RECURSIVE, MySQL doesn't allow self-referencing CTEs that would cause infinite loops.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
WITH RECURSIVE employee_hierarchy AS (
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level
    FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, eh.level + 1
    FROM employees e
    JOIN employee_hierarchy eh ON e.manager_id = eh.id
) SELECT * FROM employee_hierarchy;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always use WITH RECURSIVE when the CTE references itself.

common_mistakes.title: Infinite recursion without termination
common_mistakes.error_message: Recursive query aborted after 1000 iterations. Try increasing @@cte_max_recursion_depth
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: No termination condition - the CTE would generate numbers forever.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always include a WHERE clause in the recursive member to ensure termination.

reflection_prompts: When would you choose a CTE over a subquery? Over a temporary table?
How do recursive CTEs work 'under the hood'?
What are the performance implications of using many CTEs in one query?
How would you find all descendants in a category tree using recursive CTEs?
practice_suggestions: Build a bill of materials query using recursive CTE (product components)
Create a query finding all paths in a social network up to 3 degrees
Write a date series generator for reporting (all days in a month)
Implement a moving average calculation using CTEs

normalization.definition: Normalization is the process of organizing data to minimize redundancy and dependency. It involves dividing large tables into smaller ones and defining relationships between them. The normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF) provide progressively stricter rules for eliminating data anomalies.
normalization.why_it_matters: Proper normalization prevents data inconsistencies (update anomalies), reduces storage waste, and ensures data integrity. However, over-normalization can hurt read performance. Understanding normalization helps you design databases that balance integrity with performance, and recognize when denormalization might be appropriate.

examples.title: Unnormalized data (problems)
examples.sql:
-- Orders table with redundant customer data
CREATE TABLE bad_orders (
    order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    order_date DATE,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100),
    customer_email VARCHAR(100),
    customer_address VARCHAR(200),
    product_names VARCHAR(500),  -- 'Laptop, Mouse, Keyboard'
    total DECIMAL(10,2)
);
examples.explanation: Problems: Customer info repeats for each order. Product names concatenated in one field. Can't query individual products. Address change requires updating many rows.
examples.scenario: Anti-pattern: what NOT to do. Shows common beginner mistakes.

examples.title: Third Normal Form (3NF) design
examples.sql:
-- Separate entities, no transitive dependencies
CREATE TABLE customers (
    customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    address_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (address_id) REFERENCES addresses(address_id)
);

CREATE TABLE addresses (
    address_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    street VARCHAR(200),
    city VARCHAR(100),
    postal_code VARCHAR(20)
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INT,
    order_date DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
    order_id INT,
    product_id INT,
    quantity INT,
    price DECIMAL(10,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);
examples.explanation: Each entity has its own table. No repeating groups (1NF). No partial dependencies (2NF). No transitive dependencies (3NF). Can update address in one place.
examples.scenario: Proper e-commerce schema that scales and maintains data integrity.

examples.title: Intentional denormalization for performance
examples.sql:
-- Add calculated/denormalized column for frequent query
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN total_amount DECIMAL(10,2);

-- Maintain via trigger
CREATE TRIGGER update_order_total
AFTER INSERT ON order_items
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    UPDATE orders
    SET total_amount = (
        SELECT SUM(quantity * price)
        FROM order_items
        WHERE order_id = NEW.order_id
    )
    WHERE order_id = NEW.order_id;
END;
examples.explanation: Sometimes you intentionally denormalize (add redundancy) for performance, but you maintain it automatically via triggers to prevent anomalies.
examples.scenario: High-read scenarios where calculating totals on every query is too slow.

common_mistakes.title: Storing comma-separated values
common_mistakes.error_message: No error, but can't properly query, index, or maintain this data
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Beginners try to fit multiple values in one field to avoid 'complicated' joins.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Never store multiple values in one column. Use junction tables for many-to-many relationships.

common_mistakes.title: Over-normalization hurting performance
common_mistakes.error_sql:
-- 10-table join for a simple report
SELECT o.*, c.name, a.street, a.city, p.postal_code_name, ...
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON ...
JOIN addresses a ON ...
JOIN postal_codes p ON ...
JOIN cities ci ON ...
-- etc
common_mistakes.error_message: Slow query due to excessive joins
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Normalizing every possible attribute leads to too many joins for common queries.
common_mistakes.fix_sql:
-- Consider keeping commonly-accessed data together
CREATE TABLE order_summary AS
SELECT o.*, c.name, c.email, a.city, a.state
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON ...
JOIN addresses a ON ...;
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Normalize to 3NF, then denormalize selectively for performance based on actual query patterns.

reflection_prompts: What problems can occur when updating data in unnormalized tables?
How do you decide between normalization and performance?
When is it acceptable to violate normal forms?
What's the difference between 2NF and 3NF? Can you give an example of 2NF but not 3NF?
practice_suggestions: Normalize a spreadsheet of student course enrollments into proper tables
Design a database for a library (books, patrons, loans, fines)
Create a schema for a blog with users, posts, comments, tags, and categories
Analyze an existing database schema and identify normalization violations

foreign-key.definition: A FOREIGN KEY is a column (or set of columns) that establishes a link between data in two tables. It references the PRIMARY KEY of another table, enforcing referential integrity - ensuring relationships between tables remain valid and preventing orphaned records.
foreign-key.why_it_matters: Foreign keys are the foundation of relational databases. They prevent invalid data (like orders for non-existent customers), enable cascading operations (delete related records automatically), and document relationships in the schema itself. Without foreign keys, your 'relational' database is just a collection of disconnected spreadsheets.

examples.title: Basic foreign key with CASCADE
examples.explanation: ON DELETE SET NULL: If department is deleted, employees' dept_id becomes NULL (not deleted). ON UPDATE CASCADE: If dept_id changes, all employee references update automatically.
examples.scenario: Department restructuring: renaming or reorganizing without losing employee data.

examples.title: Foreign key with RESTRICT (prevent deletion)
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
examples.explanation: ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents deleting a customer who has orders. You must delete or reassign orders first. Protects against accidental data loss.
examples.scenario: E-commerce: Prevent deleting customers with order history for accounting compliance.

examples.title: Self-referencing foreign key
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE employees (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    manager_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (manager_id) REFERENCES employees(emp_id)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
);
examples.explanation: A foreign key can reference its own table. This creates hierarchical relationships like employee-manager. ON DELETE SET NULL handles when a manager is deleted.
examples.scenario: Organizational chart where managers are also employees in the same table.

common_mistakes.title: Referencing non-unique column
common_mistakes.error_message: Failed to add the foreign key constraint. Missing unique key for constraint
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Foreign keys must reference a PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE column to ensure exactly one match.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Foreign keys can only reference PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE columns.

common_mistakes.title: Data type mismatch between FK and PK
common_mistakes.error_message: Failed to add the foreign key constraint. Column types don't match
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: INT and VARCHAR are incompatible types, even if they contain the same-looking values.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Foreign key columns must have exactly the same data type as the referenced column.

reflection_prompts: What would happen to data integrity if you didn't use foreign keys?
When would you choose SET NULL vs CASCADE vs RESTRICT for ON DELETE?
How do foreign keys affect insert performance? Is the trade-off worth it?
Can you have a foreign key that references a composite primary key?
practice_suggestions: Design a university schema with students, courses, professors, and enrollments
Create a social media schema with users, posts, comments, and likes (all with FKs)
Implement soft delete using status flags while maintaining FK relationships
Build a product catalog with categories, subcategories, and products

primary-key.definition: A PRIMARY KEY is a column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a table. It enforces entity integrity by ensuring no duplicates and no NULL values. Every table should have a primary key - it's how you reference specific records.
primary-key.why_it_matters: Primary keys are essential for data integrity and relationships. They provide a reliable way to identify and retrieve specific rows, enable efficient indexing for fast lookups, and serve as the target for foreign key relationships. Without primary keys, you can't reliably update or delete specific rows, and relationships between tables become impossible.

examples.title: Simple auto-increment primary key
examples.explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT generates unique IDs automatically. INTEGER PKs are small, fast, and efficient for indexing and foreign key references.
examples.scenario: Standard approach for most tables: simple, fast, surrogate key.

examples.title: Composite primary key
examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE order_items (
    order_id INT,
    product_id INT,
    quantity INT NOT NULL,
    price DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders(order_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES products(product_id)
);
examples.explanation: When a single column isn't unique enough, combine columns. Here, the same order can have multiple products, and the same product can be in multiple orders, but the combination is unique.
examples.scenario: Junction tables for many-to-many relationships often use composite keys.

examples.title: Natural key as primary key
examples.explanation: Natural keys use business data as the identifier. country_code 'US' or email 'user@example.com' are meaningful and unique. No separate ID column needed.
examples.scenario: Lookup tables or when the natural identifier is stable and never changes.

common_mistakes.title: No primary key on table
common_mistakes.error_message: No immediate error, but causes problems later
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Temporary tables or quick prototypes often skip PK, causing issues with updates, replication, and ORM frameworks.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Always define a primary key, even on 'temporary' tables.

common_mistakes.title: Using unstable data as natural key
common_mistakes.error_message: Cascading updates to all foreign key references, or broken references if CASCADE not set
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Email addresses can change. Changing a primary key requires updating all foreign key references.
common_mistakes.key_takeaway: Use surrogate keys (auto-increment) when natural keys might change.

reflection_prompts: What's the difference between a primary key and a unique constraint?
Why are auto-increment integers usually preferred over UUIDs for primary keys?
When might you choose a composite primary key over a single column?
What problems can occur when updating a primary key value?
practice_suggestions: Design primary keys for: a blog posts table, a user sessions table, a product reviews table
Create a schema where all tables have appropriate primary keys and relationships
Compare query performance between tables with and without primary keys
Design a multi-tenant system where primary keys need to be unique per tenant
